source_description: Questions derived from TECHNICAL_DOCUMENTATION.md and sample_ai_agents.md shipped with DocChat.

entries.question: What embedding model does DocChat use for vector search?
entries.ground_truth_answer: DocChat uses all-MiniLM-L6-v2 with ONNX runtime via Chroma's built-in DefaultEmbeddingFunction.

entries.question: What is the chunk size used by DocChat's text splitter?
entries.ground_truth_answer: DocChat uses a chunk size of 1000 characters.

entries.question: What is the chunk overlap setting in DocChat?
entries.ground_truth_answer: The chunk overlap is set to 200 characters.

entries.question: What vector database does DocChat use?
entries.ground_truth_answer: DocChat uses ChromaDB as its vector database.

entries.question: What web framework is used for DocChat's user interface?
entries.ground_truth_answer: DocChat uses Gradio for its web user interface.

entries.question: What is the default server port for DocChat?
entries.ground_truth_answer: DocChat launches on port 7860 by default.

entries.question: What framework is used for workflow orchestration in DocChat?
entries.ground_truth_answer: DocChat uses LangGraph for orchestrating the multi-agent workflow as a directed state graph.
relevant_chunk_texts: LangGraph is a library for building stateful, multi-agent workflows as directed graphs

entries.question: How many agents does DocChat use and what are they?
entries.ground_truth_answer: DocChat uses three agents: a Relevance Checker (gatekeeper that filters irrelevant questions), a Research Agent (generates answers from context), and a Verification Agent (fact-checks answers).
relevant_chunk_texts: Relevance Checker: Filters irrelevant questions early

entries.question: What temperature setting is used for the Verification Agent?
entries.ground_truth_answer: The Verification Agent uses temperature=0 for maximum determinism in fact-checking.

entries.question: What temperature setting is used for the Research Agent?
entries.ground_truth_answer: The Research Agent uses temperature=0.3, balancing factual accuracy with fluent, natural responses.
relevant_chunk_texts: High enough for fluent, natural responses

entries.question: What file types does DocChat support for document upload?
entries.ground_truth_answer: DocChat supports PDF, DOCX, TXT, and MD file types.

entries.question: What is the maximum total file size allowed for uploads?
entries.ground_truth_answer: The maximum total size for all uploaded files is 200 MB.

entries.question: What hashing algorithm is used for document caching?
entries.ground_truth_answer: DocChat uses SHA-256 hashing for content-based caching. The same content always produces the same hash.
relevant_chunk_texts: Content-based hashing: same content = same hash

entries.question: How long is the document cache valid before expiring?
entries.ground_truth_answer: The document cache is valid for 7 days (CACHE_EXPIRE_DAYS = 7).

entries.question: What are the weights for the hybrid retriever components?
entries.ground_truth_answer: The hybrid retriever uses weights of 0.4 for BM25 (keyword search) and 0.6 for vector search (semantic search).

entries.question: What method is used for combining BM25 and vector search results?
entries.ground_truth_answer: DocChat uses Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) with a smoothing constant k=60 to combine the ranked results from BM25 and vector search.
relevant_chunk_texts: k is a smoothing constant (standard is 60)

entries.question: What library is used for PDF text extraction?
entries.ground_truth_answer: DocChat uses pypdf (PdfReader) for extracting text from PDF files.
relevant_chunk_texts: Extract text from PDF using pypdf

entries.question: What is the VECTOR_SEARCH_K parameter value?
entries.ground_truth_answer: VECTOR_SEARCH_K is set to 10, meaning the vector retriever returns the top 10 most similar documents.

entries.question: What are the three possible outputs of the Relevance Checker?
entries.ground_truth_answer: The Relevance Checker outputs one of three labels: CAN_ANSWER (passages contain enough info to fully answer), PARTIAL (passages mention topic but incomplete), or NO_MATCH (passages don't discuss the topic).
relevant_chunk_texts: Classify how well the document content addresses the question

entries.question: What split separators does the text splitter use and in what order?
How does the caching system recognize when the same file is uploaded twice?
entries.ground_truth_answer: The caching system generates a SHA-256 hash of the file content. If the same file is uploaded again, it produces the same hash, and the cached processed chunks are loaded instead of reprocessing.
relevant_chunk_texts: Content-based hashing: same content = same hash
If you upload the same file twice, it's recognized instantly

entries.question: How do the hybrid retriever weights affect which documents are ranked higher?
entries.ground_truth_answer: The weights [0.4, 0.6] mean vector search results contribute 60% to the final RRF score while BM25 contributes 40%. Documents that score high on vector (semantic) search are favored over those that only match keywords.
relevant_chunk_texts: 40% BM25: Ensures exact keywords aren't ignored

entries.question: What happens when a verification check fails in the workflow?
entries.ground_truth_answer: When verification fails (Supported: NO or Relevant: NO), the workflow triggers a self-correction loop that sends the query back to the Research Agent for regeneration. The new answer is then re-verified.

entries.question: How does chunk overlap preserve context between adjacent chunks?
entries.ground_truth_answer: With 200-character overlap, the end of one chunk and the beginning of the next share 200 characters of text. This prevents information loss at split boundaries by keeping connecting sentences in both chunks.
relevant_chunk_texts: Prevents information loss at split points

entries.question: What role does the Relevance Checker play before the Research Agent processes a question?
entries.ground_truth_answer: The Relevance Checker acts as a gatekeeper. It retrieves top-k chunks and classifies whether the question can be answered from the documents. If it returns NO_MATCH, the workflow ends early without invoking the Research Agent, saving compute.
relevant_chunk_texts: Is this question answerable from documents?

entries.question: How does DocChat handle duplicate content across multiple uploaded files?
entries.ground_truth_answer: DocChat deduplicates chunks across files by computing a SHA-256 hash of each chunk's content. If two chunks from different files have the same hash, only the first is kept.

entries.question: What is the complete path of a user's question through all three agents?
entries.ground_truth_answer: The question first goes to the Relevance Checker which classifies it. If relevant (CAN_ANSWER or PARTIAL), it proceeds to the Research Agent which generates a draft answer using retrieved context. The draft then goes to the Verification Agent which fact-checks it. If verification fails, it loops back to Research.

entries.question: How does session state prevent unnecessary reprocessing of documents?
entries.ground_truth_answer: DocChat stores the retriever and file hashes in Gradio session state. When a new question is asked, it compares current file hashes against stored hashes. If unchanged, the cached retriever is reused without reprocessing or rebuilding the vector store.
relevant_chunk_texts: if state["retriever"] is None or current_hashes != state["file_hashes"]

entries.question: Why is temperature set to 0 for the Verification Agent but 0.3 for the Research Agent?
entries.ground_truth_answer: The Verification Agent uses temperature=0 because fact-checking requires maximum determinism and consistent results — there should be no creativity in classification. The Research Agent uses temperature=0.3 to balance factual accuracy with natural, fluent response generation.
relevant_chunk_texts: High enough for fluent, natural responses

entries.question: Why does DocChat use hybrid retrieval instead of just vector search?
entries.ground_truth_answer: Neither approach is perfect alone. Vector search understands semantics and synonyms but can miss exact keywords. BM25 excels at exact keyword matching but can't understand meaning. Hybrid search combines both, getting better recall through keywords and better semantic understanding through embeddings.

entries.question: Why is chunk overlap important for retrieval accuracy?
entries.ground_truth_answer: Without overlap, information at chunk boundaries can be lost — a sentence split across two chunks loses its meaning in both. Overlap ensures connecting sentences appear in both adjacent chunks, preserving context and improving retrieval accuracy.
relevant_chunk_texts: Prevents information loss at split points

entries.question: Why is the BM25 weight (0.4) lower than the vector search weight (0.6)?
entries.ground_truth_answer: Vector search is weighted higher (0.6) because it prioritizes semantic understanding, which is more important for natural language questions. BM25 at 0.4 still ensures exact keywords are not ignored, providing a safety net for specific terms, product names, and codes.
relevant_chunk_texts: 40% BM25: Ensures exact keywords aren't ignored

entries.question: Why does the Relevance Checker use a very low max_tokens setting?
entries.ground_truth_answer: The Relevance Checker uses max_tokens=10 because its response is just a single classification label (CAN_ANSWER, PARTIAL, or NO_MATCH). This saves costs and reduces latency since no lengthy text generation is needed.

entries.question: Why is SHA-256 used for cache validation instead of file modification time?
entries.ground_truth_answer: SHA-256 provides content-based hashing, meaning the same content always produces the same hash regardless of when or where the file was created. This is more reliable than modification time because copying a file changes its timestamp but not its content.
relevant_chunk_texts: Content-based hashing: same content = same hash

entries.question: Why does the system use RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter instead of simple character splitting?
entries.ground_truth_answer: RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter preserves semantic structure by trying to split at natural boundaries first (double newlines for paragraphs, then single newlines, then sentences, then words). Simple character splitting would cut in the middle of words or sentences, destroying meaning.

entries.question: What are the differences between BM25 and vector search in terms of their strengths and weaknesses?
entries.ground_truth_answer: BM25 strengths: exact keyword matching, good for specific terms, fast, no training needed. BM25 weaknesses: can't understand synonyms, misses semantic relationships. Vector search strengths: understands meaning and context, handles synonyms, semantic similarity. Vector search weaknesses: can miss exact keywords, computationally expensive, requires quality embeddings.

entries.question: How does the Research Agent differ from the Verification Agent in LLM configuration?
entries.ground_truth_answer: The Research Agent uses temperature=0.3 and max_tokens=800 for generating fluent, detailed answers. The Verification Agent uses temperature=0.0 and max_tokens=500 for deterministic, structured fact-checking output.

entries.question: What is the difference between CAN_ANSWER and PARTIAL relevance classifications?
entries.ground_truth_answer: CAN_ANSWER means the passages contain enough explicit information to fully answer the question. PARTIAL means the passages mention or discuss the topic but don't provide all the details needed for a complete answer.
relevant_chunk_texts: CAN_ANSWER: The passages contain enough explicit information to fully answer
PARTIAL: The passages mention or discuss the question's topic but do not provide all details

entries.question: Compare the document processing approaches for PDF files versus plain text files.
entries.ground_truth_answer: PDF files require a dedicated library (pypdf/PdfReader) to extract text page by page, joining pages with double newlines. Text and markdown files are read directly with Python's built-in open() using UTF-8 encoding with error ignoring.
relevant_chunk_texts: Extract text from PDF using pypdf
Simple text files - just read directly

entries.question: What is the difference between document-level caching and session-level caching in DocChat?
entries.ground_truth_answer: Document-level caching stores processed chunks as pickle files keyed by SHA-256 hash in the document_cache directory, expiring after 7 days. Session-level caching stores the built retriever in Gradio session state, expiring when the session ends, avoiding retriever reconstruction for repeated questions.

entries.question: What is the current stock price of Apple?
What is the weather forecast for tomorrow in New York?
How do I cook pasta carbonara?
Who won the FIFA World Cup in 2022?
What is the speed of light in kilometers per second?
What are the exact API cost breakdowns per query for DocChat?
What are the benchmark accuracy scores for the all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embedding model?
What load testing and stress testing results has DocChat achieved in production?
What database does DocChat use to persist BM25 indexes?
entries.ground_truth_answer: DocChat does not persist BM25 indexes to any database. The BM25 retriever is built in-memory from documents each time using BM25Retriever.from_documents().

entries.question: What are AI agents and what types exist?
entries.ground_truth_answer: AI agents are autonomous software systems that perceive their environment, make decisions, and take actions to achieve goals. Types include: Reactive agents (stimulus-response, no memory), Deliberative agents (internal model, planning), and Hybrid agents (combine reactive and deliberative).
relevant_chunk_texts: AI agents are autonomous software systems
